Chapter 10 — Regression (R companion)

R/tidyverse translation of Understanding Data by Alan Garfinkel and Yina Guo

Adrian Soto-Mota, MD, PhD, FACP.

2026-04-24

Orientation

Chapter 10 (book pp. 509–556) goes from correlation to regression: fitting a line (or curve) and using it to predict. The book contains no Python code listings in this chapter. It covers OLS, absolute-value, and orthogonal regression, then moves to logistic and spline regression. We implement OLS from scratch, then use R’s lm() / glm() for the rest.

§10.1 — Purpose of regression (book p. 509)

We want a rule y ≈ f(x). Three uses:

  1. Describe the trend.
  2. Predict y at a new x.
  3. Test whether the slope differs from zero.
n <- 100
x <- runif(n, 0, 10)
y <- 2 + 1.5 * x + rnorm(n, sd = 2)
xy <- tibble(x = x, y = y)

ggplot(xy, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Bivariate data with a linear trend")

§10.2 — Linear regression (book p. 515)

Ordinary Least Squares by hand (book §10.2 “Ordinary Least Squares regression”, p. 517)

The OLS solution minimizes the sum of squared vertical residuals:

\[\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}, \quad \hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}\]

beta1_hat <- sum((x - mean(x)) * (y - mean(y))) /
             sum((x - mean(x))^2)
beta0_hat <- mean(y) - beta1_hat * mean(x)
c(intercept = beta0_hat, slope = beta1_hat)
## intercept     slope 
##  1.492386  1.587146

Check against lm():

coef(lm(y ~ x))
## (Intercept)           x 
##    1.492386    1.587146

Plot the fit

ggplot(xy, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_abline(intercept = beta0_hat, slope = beta1_hat,
              color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = sprintf("OLS fit: y = %.2f + %.2f × x", beta0_hat, beta1_hat))

When is vertical distance the right choice? (book §10.2 “When is it OK to use vertical distances?” and “Orthogonal regression”, pp. 516, 525)

Vertical distance makes sense when x is measured precisely (or chosen) and only y has error. When both have measurement error, OLS is biased and you should use orthogonal regression:

# Orthogonal (total least squares) via principal component
xc <- scale(xy$x, scale = FALSE)
yc <- scale(xy$y, scale = FALSE)
pc <- prcomp(cbind(xc, yc))

v          <- pc$rotation[, 1]            # first principal component
slope_orth <- v[2] / v[1]
intercept_orth <- mean(y) - slope_orth * mean(x)
c(intercept_orth = intercept_orth, slope_orth = slope_orth)
## intercept_orth     slope_orth 
##       0.587033       1.759766

Absolute-value (L1) regression (book §10.2, p. 524)

Minimize Σ|y − (β₀ + β₁x)| — robust to outliers.

library(quantreg)   # install.packages("quantreg")
rq_fit <- rq(y ~ x, tau = 0.5)   # median regression = L1
coef(rq_fit)
## (Intercept)           x 
##    1.480865    1.621734
methods <- tibble(
  method    = c("OLS (vertical)", "Orthogonal", "L1 (median)"),
  intercept = c(beta0_hat, intercept_orth, coef(rq_fit)[1]),
  slope     = c(beta1_hat, slope_orth,     coef(rq_fit)[2])
)
methods
## # A tibble: 3 x 3
##   method         intercept slope
##   <chr>              <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 OLS (vertical)     1.49   1.59
## 2 Orthogonal         0.587  1.76
## 3 L1 (median)        1.48   1.62

With clean Normal data they agree. With outliers, L1 will diverge from OLS (exercise 1).

§10.3 — Nonlinear regression (book p. 527)

Logistic regression (book §10.3, p. 527)

For binary outcomes. Each observation is 0/1; model the probability as 1 / (1 + exp(−(β₀ + β₁x))).

set.seed(1)
n <- 200
x <- runif(n, -3, 3)
logit_p <- -1 + 2 * x
p <- plogis(logit_p)                  # logistic function
y <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = p)    # binary outcome

log_df <- tibble(x = x, y = y)

fit_log <- glm(y ~ x, data = log_df, family = binomial)
summary(fit_log)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = y ~ x, family = binomial, data = log_df)
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)  -0.4728     0.2462  -1.921   0.0548 .  
## x             2.0680     0.2862   7.227 4.95e-13 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 276.94  on 199  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 114.84  on 198  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 118.84
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6
pred <- tibble(x = seq(-3, 3, 0.1))
pred$p <- predict(fit_log, newdata = pred, type = "response")

ggplot(log_df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_jitter(height = 0.05, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(data = pred, aes(x, p), color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Logistic regression: P(y = 1 | x)", y = "P(y = 1)")

Spline regression (book §10.3, p. 533)

When the relationship is curvy but you don’t know the functional form.

n <- 200
x <- sort(runif(n, 0, 10))
y <- sin(x) + 0.1 * x + rnorm(n, sd = 0.3)

spline_df <- tibble(x = x, y = y)

# Natural cubic spline with 6 knots
library(splines)
fit_spline <- lm(y ~ ns(x, df = 6), data = spline_df)
spline_df$yhat <- predict(fit_spline)

ggplot(spline_df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(aes(y = yhat), color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Spline regression (df = 6)")

§10.4 — Significance and CIs for the slope (book p. 537)

Permutation test

# Use the first (linear) example again
xy <- tibble(x = runif(100, 0, 10),
             y = NA)
xy$y <- 0.3 * xy$x + rnorm(100, sd = 3)   # weak true slope

obs_slope <- coef(lm(y ~ x, data = xy))[2]

n_sim <- 5000
null_slope <- numeric(n_sim)
for (i in seq_len(n_sim)) {
  shuffled <- xy
  shuffled$y <- sample(xy$y)             # break x–y link
  null_slope[i] <- coef(lm(y ~ x, data = shuffled))[2]
}

p_value <- mean(abs(null_slope) >= abs(obs_slope))
p_value
## [1] 0.005

Bootstrap CI for the slope

B <- 10000
boot_slope <- numeric(B)
for (i in seq_len(B)) {
  idx <- sample(seq_len(nrow(xy)), nrow(xy), replace = TRUE)
  boot_slope[i] <- coef(lm(y ~ x, data = xy[idx, ]))[2]
}
quantile(boot_slope, c(0.025, 0.975))
##       2.5%      97.5% 
## 0.08304094 0.51686719

Compare to the parametric CI:

confint(lm(y ~ x, data = xy), "x")
##        2.5 %    97.5 %
## x 0.08858161 0.5208472

Wildfires example (book §10.4 “Are wildfires increasing in CA?”, p. 540)

# Chapter's question: are California wildfires increasing?
# Simulated annual-burned-acres series:
years <- 1970:2025
acres <- 50000 + 3000 * (years - 1970) + rnorm(length(years), sd = 80000)
fires <- tibble(year = years, acres = acres)

fit <- lm(acres ~ year, data = fires)
summary(fit)$coefficients
##                Estimate   Std. Error   t value     Pr(>|t|)
## (Intercept) -7001802.69 1243215.8432 -5.632009 6.587704e-07
## year            3576.65     622.3655  5.746864 4.327953e-07
confint(fit)
##                    2.5 %       97.5 %
## (Intercept) -9494300.385 -4509304.995
## year            2328.882     4824.418
ggplot(fires, aes(year, acres)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "California acres burned, 1970–2025 (simulated)")

A significant positive slope with a CI excluding zero: consistent with an increasing trend. But a LINEAR model may be too simple — try a spline and compare:

fit_sp <- lm(acres ~ ns(year, df = 4), data = fires)
fires$linear <- predict(fit,    newdata = fires)
fires$spline <- predict(fit_sp, newdata = fires)

ggplot(fires, aes(year, acres)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(aes(y = linear), color = "red") +
  geom_line(aes(y = spline), color = "blue", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Linear (red) vs. spline (blue) fits")

The spline reveals whether the trend is linear, accelerating, or decelerating — information the linear fit hides.

Exercises

  1. Add 5 outliers at large x, small y to the linear dataset. Re-fit OLS, L1, and orthogonal. Which methods move the most? Which reflects the “honest” relationship?
  2. For the logistic fit, compute the x at which P(y = 1) = 0.5. (Hint: set β₀ + β₁x = 0.) Add a vertical line at that x to the plot.
  3. Fit splines with df = 3, 6, 12, 20 to the sine example. Plot all four. Discuss the bias-variance trade-off (under/over-fitting).
